arrays.name: Arrays
arrays.tagline: Contiguous memory, index access, and the workhorse of most interview problems.

blocks.text: An array stores elements in a fixed sequence. Each position has an index (usually starting at 0), so you can read or overwrite any slot in constant time if you already know the index.
Most DSA problems do not ask you to implement an array — they ask you to exploit its shape: sorted order, duplicate values, fixed size, or the fact that removing from the middle is expensive.

items: Access by index — O(1)
Scan left to right — O(n)
Insert or delete in the middle — O(n) because elements shift
Sort once, then binary search — O(n log n) setup, O(log n) queries
Input is sorted or can be sorted — binary search, two pointers
Need a running aggregate over a range — prefix sums, sliding window
Count or group by value with small domain — counting sort, bucket ideas
Answer lives in a subarray or subsequence — expand/shrink a window

blocks.text: When you see an array, ask: is order important? Can I sort? Do I need every pair or only a contiguous block?

strings.name: Strings
strings.tagline: Sequences of characters where pointers, windows, and hashing show up constantly.

blocks.text: A string is an ordered sequence of characters. In interviews it behaves like an array with extra constraints: palindromes, anagrams, prefixes, and character frequency maps appear all the time.

items: Character access by index — O(1)
Substring search (naive) — O(n × m)
Build frequency map — O(n)
Two pointers from both ends — palindrome checks, pair matching
Anagram or permutation — sort or count characters
Longest / shortest valid substring — sliding window with a frequency map
Palindrome — two pointers inward, or expand around center
Pattern matching — rolling hash or KMP for advanced cases

linked-lists.name: Linked Lists
linked-lists.tagline: Nodes chained by pointers — reversal, cycle detection, and merge patterns.

blocks.text: Each node stores a value and a reference to the next node (and sometimes the previous). There is no random access: reaching index k costs O(k) steps.
The trade-off is flexible insertion and deletion at a known position — O(1) if you already hold the pointer — without shifting the rest of the structure.

items: Traverse from head — O(n)
Insert or delete at known node — O(1)
Find k-th node — O(k)
Reverse in place — three pointers: prev, curr, next
Cycle in the list — fast and slow pointers (Floyd)
Merge two sorted lists — dummy head, compare nodes
Find middle — fast pointer moves twice as fast
Reorder or reverse a segment — in-place pointer rewiring

trees.name: Trees
trees.tagline: Hierarchical data — recursion, depth, and path questions on every branch.

blocks.text: A tree is a connected acyclic graph with a root. Binary trees restrict each node to at most two children. Many problems reduce to: what do I do at this node, and what do I get back from the left and right subtrees?

items: DFS (pre/in/post-order) — go deep first, natural for recursion
BFS (level order) — queue, process layer by layer
Height / depth — max edges from root, or from root to a node
Validate BST — pass valid (min, max) range down the tree
Path sum or path existence — DFS with running total
Lowest common ancestor — post-order or parent pointers
Serialize / deserialize — preorder with null markers
Diameter or max path — combine left and right results at each node

graphs.name: Graphs
graphs.tagline: Nodes and edges — reachability, shortest paths, and dependency order.

blocks.text: A graph is a set of vertices connected by edges. It may be directed or undirected, weighted or unweighted. Grids, social networks, course prerequisites, and islands on a matrix are all graphs in disguise.

items: BFS — shortest path in unweighted graphs, level by level
DFS — explore all reachable nodes, detect cycles
Topological sort — ordering with dependencies (DAG only)
Union-Find — dynamic connectivity, counting components
Can I reach X from Y? — BFS or DFS with a visited set
Minimum steps or shortest path — BFS on implicit grid graph
Course schedule / build order — topological sort
Number of islands — DFS/BFS flood fill on matrix

heaps.name: Heaps
heaps.tagline: Always know the min or max — top K, merge K lists, and running medians.

blocks.text: A binary heap is a complete binary tree stored in an array where every parent is smaller (min-heap) or larger (max-heap) than its children. The root always holds the extreme value.

items: Peek min or max — O(1)
Insert — O(log n) bubble up
Extract min or max — O(log n) bubble down
Heapify an array — O(n)
Top K largest or smallest — size-K heap beats full sort
Merge K sorted lists — min-heap of list heads
Running median — two heaps (max-heap for lower half, min-heap for upper)
Schedule tasks with cooldown — heap for next available time

matrix.name: Matrix
matrix.tagline: 2D grids — neighbors, directions, and graph traversal on rows and columns.

blocks.text: A matrix is a 2D array: rows and columns. Each cell (r, c) often connects to up to four neighbors. Most grid problems are really graph traversal with careful boundary checks.

items: Row/column scan — O(rows × cols)
DFS/BFS flood fill — mark visited, explore neighbors
Prefix sum on submatrix — build 2D prefix for range queries
Rotate or transpose — index math, often in-place layers
Count islands or connected components — DFS from each unvisited 1
Shortest path in grid — BFS, not DFS
Search in sorted matrix — treat as graph or use binary search on rows
Game board / word search — backtracking with visited marks

math.name: Math
math.tagline: Modular arithmetic, primes, and number tricks that unlock clean solutions.

blocks.text: Math problems are not about memorizing formulas — they are about spotting structure: divisibility, remainders, XOR cancellation, combinatorics with constraints, or reducing brute force with number theory.

items: Modulo — keep numbers bounded, detect cycles in sequences
GCD / LCM — Euclidean algorithm
Prime sieve — precompute primes up to n
XOR — a ^ a = 0, pairs cancel; find the lone element
Huge numbers — work modulo 10^9 + 7
Single number among pairs — XOR all values
Count ways with overflow risk — DP + modulo
Palindrome number or reverse digits — digit extraction math
